titleStmt.title: Introduction to Stele of Haitan Monastery in Dongping County

figure.head: Panoramic view of the Haitan Monastery stele among cultivated terraces; photograph taken in 2006
海檀寺碑與農作梯田的全景，2006年照片

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 海檀寺遺址位於山東省中部山地西端的一個東南偏東走向的山谷中，距東平縣城西北17公里、平陰縣城西南偏南方30公里（見封二地圖）。在此山谷中，農作梯田高達4米（）。谷底海拔約160米。
海檀寺内建築的原始位置和方位已不明，不過直至2006年，在寺院遺址內仍保存有兩塊石碑和一些石質構件殘塊（）。寺院遺址北側有一塊明代的石碑；南側有一塊面向東南偏南方、紀年為北齊皇建元年（560）的石碑（地圖，）。 其準確的地理位置為東經116.3025°，北緯36.0296°，相當於UTM坐標系統50S帶，東行437160，北行3987460。
洪頂山刻經位於海檀寺西北6.9公里處，但是不在此處的視線範圍之內。雲翠山刻經距此西北偏北3.8公里，雖然雲翠山和海檀寺坐落在同一條南北走向的山脊連線上，但它們並不在彼此的視野內。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 海檀寺的面積相當小，其遺址從東到西只有60米，從北至南26米。在北區靠近山腳處，堆積了從北朝至後代的磗塊，證實了前寺廟建築的存在。賴非 191。在遺址南側耕作梯田的石墻中保存有一個柱礎（）；距此不遠處立有一塊刻著《觀世音經》的石碑，其紀年為北齊皇建元年（560）（）。這塊石碑很可能埋在地下許久，一直到明成化年間（1465-1487）才被發現而重新豎立起來。 楊浩、郝莉 178。 這塊碑於2006年9月被遷移，下落不明。根據賴非於東方學會2009年5月15-16日在東京舉辦的東方學國際研討會上提供的信息。 在寺院遺址的中部，有一塊238釐米高、112釐米寬、34釐米厚的成化年間（1465-1487）的石碑（）。其正面嚴重風化的碑文冠有“書堂寺鼎建正殿造塑像立碑記”的碑名。 碑陰有明代性覺寺、觀音堂、堯帝崇聖寺、延慶寺和報恩寺住持撰寫的題記。在該碑西側的山岩上，有一幅160釐米高的線刻，上有一個坐落在蓮座上的佛塔中的佛字（）。 楊浩、郝莉 178。
在小望山前，距遺址約0.7公里處，三座明代僧人的靈骨塔被重新豎立了起來。賴非 191。其中的兩座可以確定為書堂寺僧人圓珍和覺怒的靈骨塔。塔高均為12米，保存狀況良好。楊浩、郝莉 178。

div.head: 釋名
div.p: 刻有《觀世音經》的石碑保存了該寺院在北朝時的名字──海檀寺。從明代的石碑可知，在成化年間（1465-1487）這裡被稱作書堂寺。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 《觀世音經》碑背面的願文（）記錄了比丘法慧以及其他五十幾名邑社成員的名字，這些人均不見載籍。
此外，碑側還記錄有碑陽像龕內每尊造像的供養人姓名。賴非162。楊浩認為，訓堂主、施地主和維那亦列名其上。楊浩、郝莉176。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 《觀世音經》碑背面的願文也明確記載了刊刻的年代，即北齊皇建元年（560）。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 最早由吳式芬（1796-1856）在他的《金石彙目分編》中提及石碑的位置並著錄了正面及背面的碑名。《金石彙目分編》1, 28: 21218。 之後，繆荃孫（1844-1919）在編輯其1898年作序的《藝風堂金石文字目》時，參考了吳的記錄。《藝風堂金石文字目》1, 26: 19549。 至今，該碑在山東省石刻藝術博物館於2003、2006年出版的《北朝摩崖刻經研究》第二、三輯得到了近一步的討論。 此外，顏娟英2008年出版的一冊收藏於中央研究院的拓片，收錄了該碑背面的拓片及錄文。見本卷海檀寺2號。

figure.head: 寺院遺址中部及明代石碑，2006年照片
Middle part of the former monastery grounds with the Ming dynasty stele; photograph taken in 2006
海檀寺遺址地圖
Map of the ruins of Haitan Monastery

div.head: 銘文總數
div.p: 一篇經文(海檀寺1號)、兩篇歷史題記（海檀寺2號和海檀寺3號）。

div.head: 海檀寺1號
div.p: 碑陽上方有三小龕（）。每龕高約35.5釐米、寬約29釐米。碑上左龕主尊為一坐佛，兩側各有三脅侍。中龕主尊為倚坐觀世音菩薩，其側各有一脅侍。右龕主尊佛結跏趺坐於一高壇之上，其旁有數位脅侍；壇前風化嚴重，獅子、人物及香爐僅依稀可見輪廓；此龕兩旁柱子由夜叉承托。

經文鐫刻於碑正面的三個像龕之下，與《大正藏》收錄的鳩摩羅什所譯《妙法蓮華經》第二十五品──“觀世音菩薩普門品”一致，只是省略偈頌部分，仲崇霖 298。這一點與鳩摩羅什譯本原貌一致。 于君方 38，對此解釋，《妙法蓮華經》截取闍那崛多和達摩笈多完成於公元601年的譯本 (T#264, 9: 134-197) 中的品末偈頌，再添加到較之更早、更流行的鳩摩羅什譯本。 這篇經文也被冠以《觀音經》之名單獨流傳。

div.head: 海檀寺2號
div.p: 碑陰願文冠以“大齊東平郡須昌縣海檀寺”之名。碑文可分為四部分：第一部分為一長篇的引文，用基本押韻的文字闡述了佛陀最精妙的原則之表現及其教誨。 第二部分陳說供養人──僧人法慧建造此鎸有佛像和觀音經的石碑，以祈願所有佛教邑社成員的正覺及在龍華樹下的得道。 下一部分由三段各四行的偈言組成，表讚刻經活動為促進佛教教義的適當手段。最後，願文的末段記錄了年代。
在題記下方和石碑兩側列有捐贈者姓名，但這些名字大都已模糊不清、不易釋讀。楊浩、郝莉 175計數出將近一百位供養人。賴非162和楊浩、郝莉176列舉出的碑側供養人名單完全不同。

div.head: 海檀寺3號
div.p: 碑陰載該碑於明成化年間（1465-1487）重立。

figure.head: 寺院南區，緊靠《觀世音經》碑的石牆中的柱礎，2006年照片

Pillar base in stone wall close to the stele with the
Guanshiyin Sutra , in the southern part of the monastery grounds; photograph taken in 2006

div.head: Haitan Monastery Stele in Dongping County
Location
div.p: The ruins of the former Haitan Monastery 海檀寺 are located in a valley running east-southeast at the western end of Shandong’s central ranges, 17 km to the northwest of the county seat of Dongping 東平, and 30 km south-southwest of the county seat of Pingyin 平陰 (map in front endpaper). In this valley, terraces were built, up to 4 m in height, for cultivation (fig. ). The valley’s base is about 160 m above sea level.

The original position and orientation of Haitan Monastery is unknown, but in 2006, two stelae and some stone artefacts were still extant in the former monastery grounds (fig. ). In the northern part, a stele from the Ming dynasty is located. A stele dated to the first year of the huangjian era 皇建 (560) of the Northern Qi dynasty was found in the southern part of the former monastery, facing south-southeast (map, fig. ).
Geographically, the southern stele is situated 116.3025° east and 36.0296° north WGS84. This equals 437160 E (easting) and 3987460 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM.

The inscriptions of Mount Hongding 洪頂山 lie 6.9 km to the northwest of Haitan Monastery. These inscriptions are not within the line of sight. The inscriptions of Mount Yuncui 雲翠山 are situated 3.8 km to the north-northwest. Although both locations are connected by a north-south oriented mountain range, they are not within each other’s line of sight.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p:
The Haitan Monastery was probably limited in size and its ruins cover an area measuring only 60 m from east to west, and 26 m from north to south. In the northern part, near to the foot of the mountain, bricks from the Northern dynasties and from later times were found, testifying to former monastery buildings. 191. In the southern part of the ruins, a pillar base remained in the stone wall of a terrace (fig. ); not far away stood a stele carved with the
Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds ( Guanshiyin jing
觀世音經) and dated to the first year of the huangjian era 皇建 (560) of the Northern Qi dynasty (fig. ). This stele had probably been buried for a long time and was only recovered and re-erected during the Chenghua era of the Ming dynasty (1465–1487). 178. Sometime after September 2006, the stele was moved to an unknown location.Information given by Lai Fei at the
International Conference of Eastern Studies organized by the Tōhō Gakkai 東方學會 (The Institute of Eastern Culture) in Tōkyō, May 15 to 16, 2009. In the middle part of the monastery grounds is a stele measuring 238 cm in height, 112 cm in width and 34 cm in depth from the Chenghua 成化 era (1465–1487) (fig. ). Its badly weathered inscription on the front side is entitled Report on Erecting a Stele on the Occasion of Making a Sculpted Image for the Newly Built Main Hall of Shutang Monastery (Shutangsi dingjian zhengdian zao suxiang libei ji
書堂寺鼎建正殿造塑像立碑記). On the rear, are colophons by Ming abbots of Xingjue Monastery 性覺寺, of Guanyin Hall 觀音堂, of Yaodi Chongsheng Monastery 堯帝崇聖寺, Yanqing Monastery 延慶寺, and Bao’en Monastery 報恩寺. To the west of the Ming stele, the character fo 佛, in a stupa placed on a lotus throne, has been cut in outline into the rock. It is 160 cm in height (fig. ). 178.

Three Ming dynasty tomb pagodas for monks have been erected in front of Mount Xiao Wang 小望山, at a distance of about 0.7 km from the ruins. 191. Two of these are identified as the tombs of monk Yuanzhen 圓珍 and monk Juenu 覺怒 from Shutang Monastery; they are 12 m high and well preserved. 178.

figure.head: 《觀世音經》碑上的像龕，2006年照片
Image niches of the stele with the Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds ; photograph taken in 2006

div.head: Place Name
div.p: The stele with the carved Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds preserves the name of the monastery at the time of the Northern dynasties, Haitan Si 海檀寺. The Ming stele explains that during the Chenghua 成化 era [1465–1487], this was the site of Shutang Monastery 書堂寺.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: The votive inscription on the rear of the stele with the Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (fig. ) gives the name of a monk, bhikṣu Fahui 法慧, as well as more than fifty names of members of a lay society that cannot be traced to any other historical source. Names of the donors of every single image in the niches on the front side of the stele are recorded on the lateral side of the stele, too. 162. According to Yang Hao, even the names of Donor of Teaching Hall 訓堂主, Donor of Land 施地, and a certain Project Coordinator 維那 were documented. 176.

div.head: Dating
div.p:
The votive inscription on the rear of the stele dates the carving of the
Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds to the third day of the ninth month of the first year of the huangjian 皇建 era of the Northern Qi dynasty (October 7, 560).

div.head: Research History
div.p:
Wu Shifen 吳式芬 (1796–1856) first mentioned the location of the stele and quoted the inscription titles on the front and rear sides in his Categorized Edition of an Assembled Catalog of Epigraphica
金石彙目分編. 1, 28: 21218. Miao Quansun 繆荃孫 (1844–1919) later referred to him when he listed the stele in his Catalog of Inscriptions in Metal and Stone from Yifeng Hall
藝風堂金石文字目,
1, 26: 19549. which carries a preface dated 1898. The stele was recently discussed in the second and third volumes of Research on the Sutras Engraved on Polished Cliffs of the Northern Dynasties (Beichao moya kejing yanjiu 北朝摩崖刻經研究) published in 2003 and 2006. In 2008, Yen Chuan-ying 顏娟英 published a rubbing of the stele’s inscription on the rear, with a transcription, HT 2, in a volume on rubbings of the Northern dynasties held in the Academia Sinica 中央研究院, Taibei.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: One sutra passage (HT 1) and two historical inscriptions (HT 2 and HT 3).

div.head: HT 1
div.p: The upper part of the stele’s front side is decorated with three niches, each measuring about 35.5 x 29 cm (fig. ). The right niche contains a meditating Buddha with three attending figures to each side. In the middle niche, a seated Bodhisattva, Guanshiyin, is attended by two standing bodhisattvas. The niche to the left has a meditating Buddha, attended by several standing figures. In front of the Buddha’s altar, the weathered outlines of two lions, human figures, and a censer are still visible. This niche is also framed by columns, which are supported by demons.

The sutra passage was carved on the front of the stele, underneath the three image niches. It is the twenty-fifth chapter of the Lotus Sutra , the “Universal Gate of Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds” 觀世音菩薩普門品 in Kumārajīva’s translation, as contained in the modern
Taishō
edition, but it leaves out the gatha, 298. in accordance with the original version of Kumāra­jīva’s translation. 38 explains that the verse sections at the end of each chapter were taken from the later translation done in 601 by Jñānagupta and Dharmagupta (T#264, 9: 134-197) and added to the earlier, more popular Kumārajīva text. This text was also circulated separately under the title Sutra on the Perceiver of the Sounds ( Guanyin jing
觀音經).

div.head: HT 2
div.p: The votive inscription on the rear of the stele is entitled Stele of [Hai]tan Monastery from the Great Qi [dynasty] in Xuchang County, Dongping Prefecture (Da Qi Dongping Jun Xuchang Xian [Hai]tan Si bei 大亝東平郡須昌縣[海]檀寺碑). The text can be divided into four parts: First, a lenghty introduction, more or less in parallel prose, elaborates on the fundamentals of the Buddha’s teachings. In the second part, the donor is named, a certain monk Fahui 法慧, who set up the stele carved with images and the text of the Guanshiyin Sutra , accompanied by prayers for the true awakening of all members of the Buddhist association, to be accomplished under the dragon-flower tree. The next part consists of three verses of four lines each, praising the carving of the scripture as an suitable means to promote the Buddhist teaching. The last part of the inscription gives the date.

Beneath the inscription and on both sides of the stele, the names of donors are listed. They are to a large extent effaced and not legible. 175, counted almost 100 donors. 162, and 176, convey two totally different transcriptions of the names on both sides of the stele.

div.head: HT 3
div.p: This inscription on the stele’s back side states that it was re-erected during the Chenghua era of the Ming dynasty (1465–1487).

figure.head: 位於蓮座上佛塔中的佛字石刻，2006年照片
Rock carving of the character fo in a stupa placed on a lotus throne; photograph taken in 2006
